    Galaxy Mergers

    Galaxy Mergers

    Type:Foundational concept
    Timescale:~0.1–2 Gyr (depending on mass ratio and orbit)
    Related: Collisional ring galaxies
    Star formation
    Dynamical stellar displacement
    Red sequence / Green valley / Blue cloud

    Galaxy mergers are gravitational encounters between two or more galaxies that result in their eventual coalescence into a single system. Mergers are a key driver of galaxy evolution, transforming galaxy morphology, triggering star formation, redistributing stellar populations, and building galaxy mass hierarchically[1][2].

    Major vs Minor Mergers

    Mergers are classified by the mass (or luminosity) ratio of the interacting galaxies[3]:

    • Major mergers (mass ratio ≥1:4): Both galaxies are of comparable mass. These produce the strongest tidal features, most dramatic morphological transformations, and greatest enhancement of star formation[2][4][5].
    • Minor mergers (mass ratio <1:4): A smaller satellite is accreted by a larger host. These can thicken disks, build stellar halos, and create tidal streams without destroying the primary disk[1].

    The "Pika's Halo" system hosting SN 2025adpq involves two galaxies with luminosity ratio L2/L1 ~ 0.8, making it a clear major merger[3][6].

    Tidal Structures and Morphological Signatures

    Mergers produce a variety of transient structures including tidal tails, bridges, shells, and — in the case of near head-on encounters — collisional rings[7][8]. The strongest tidal features arise in major mergers during the brief merging phase (≤0.5 Gyr)[9][10][11]. These structures offer unique laboratories for studying star formation in displaced gas and the dynamical displacement of pre-existing stellar populations[12][6].

    Star Formation Enhancement

    Galaxy mergers can trigger substantial bursts of star formation by compressing gas through tidal torques and collisional shocks[13][14][11]. In collisional ring galaxies, the propagating density wave sweeps up gas and triggers star formation at the ring front, producing SFRs of ~1 M☉/yr even in modest-mass systems[15][6]. Mergers can also temporarily rejuvenate quiescent massive galaxies that would otherwise reside on the red sequence[6].

    Impact on Supernova Populations

    Merger-triggered star formation enhances core-collapse supernova rates[16][17][18]. However, mergers also redistribute pre-existing old stellar populations to large radii through gravitational stripping, enabling Type Ia supernovae at large galactocentric offsets[6][19][20]. This dynamical displacement creates a new channel for producing large-offset and hostless supernovae[6].

    Why This Matters

    Galaxy mergers are one of the primary drivers of galaxy evolution, transforming disks into ellipticals, building stellar mass, and connecting star formation history to large-scale structure. The discovery that mergers can produce Type Ia SNe at large offsets via dynamical displacement of old progenitors[6] has implications for cosmological surveys that rely on accurate host-galaxy associations for distance measurements.

    See Also

    • Collisional ring galaxies
    • Dynamical stellar displacement
    • Star formation
    • Type Ia supernovae
    • Red sequence / Green valley / Blue cloud
    • O'Connor et al. (2026) — SN 2025adpq

    References
    1. Toomre, A. (1978). "Encounter geometry effects on galaxy interactions."
    2. Struck, C. (2010). "Physical impacts of interactions on galaxy structure."
    3. Lambas, D. G. et al. (2012). "Major/minor merger definitions by mass ratio."
    4. Wu, X. & Jiang, Y. (2012). "Numerical simulations of major merger features." ApJ, 745, 105. DOI:10.1088/0004-637X/745/2/105
    5. Fiacconi, D. et al. (2012). "Strong tidal features in major mergers."
    6. O'Connor, B. et al. (2026). "SN 2025adpq." arXiv:2603.15899
    7. Lynds, R. & Toomre, A. (1976). "On the interpretation of ring galaxies." ApJ, 209, 382.
    8. Appleton, P. N. & Struck-Marcell, C. (1996). "Collisional ring galaxies." Fund. Cosmic Phys., 16, 111.
    9. Elagali, A. et al. (2018). "Brief merging phase ≤0.5 Gyr."
    10. Lotz, J. M. et al. (2008). "Merger timescales and phases."
    11. Renaud, F. et al. (2018). "Ring lifetime ≤0.5 Gyr, enhanced SF in mergers."
    12. Hernquist, L. & Weil, M. L. (1993). "Numerical simulations of stellar displacement."
    13. Di Matteo, P. et al. (2007). "Star formation enhancement during mergers."
    14. Di Matteo, P. et al. (2008). "Merger-driven star formation."
    15. Appleton, P. N. & Struck-Marcell, C. (1987). "Enhanced star formation in collisional rings."
    16. Habergham, S. M. et al. (2010). "Enhanced CC SN rates in disturbed galaxies." ApJ, 717, 342.
    17. Habergham, S. M. et al. (2012). "CC SNe in interacting galaxies." MNRAS, 424, 2841.
    18. Michalowski, M. J. et al. (2020). "Core-collapse SNe in interacting systems."
    19. Schweizer, F. et al. (2008). "Type Ia SNe in tidal structures." AJ, 136, 1482.
    20. Ferretti, R. et al. (2017). "Large-offset Type Ia SNe in tidal features."
